ground_truth.title: How To Take Care of Your Oral Health
ground_truth.main_content:
Problems with your teeth, gums, or other parts of the mouth can increase your risk of having some diseases or health conditions. Small steps, like seeing a dentist regularly for checkups, can help you protect your mouth and teeth and live well at any age.

The A Healthy Mouth for Every Body campaign offers a variety of materials to help you and your family take good care of your oral health.

Oral health topics

Oral health for adults
Maintaining good oral health affects your well-being and quality of life.
Explore ways to maintain good oral health.

Oral health for young children
Having good oral health early in life sets your child up for good oral health as they grow.
Learn best practices for caring for your child's oral health.

Dry mouth
Having dry mouth all or most of the time is not only uncomfortable—it can lead to serious health problems.
Explore causes of dry mouth and treatment options.

People with HIV
Good oral health is an important part of managing HIV and your overall health. Make oral health care a regular part of taking care of yourself.
Learn ways to care for your oral health if you're living with HIV.

Oral health and nutrition
Consuming healthy foods and drinks can help you maintain good oral health.
Find out how nutrition affects your oral health and overall health.

Oral health and pregnancy
Maintaining good oral health is important during pregnancy or if you're planning a pregnancy.
Learn how to take care of your oral health while pregnant.

Educational and promotional resources
View educational resources on the important ways to maintain good oral health. Use these free outreach tools to help spread the word.
Explore and download free materials and outreach tools.

Facts
Periodontal (gum) disease is the most common cause of tooth loss among adults. It affects about 46% of adults ages 30 or older; about 9% of adults have severe periodontal disease.1

Between 2015 and 2018, 13% of children ages 5 to 19 in the United States had untreated cavities in their teeth.
1 in 4 adults ages 20 to 64 currently has cavities.2
In 2020, more than 54,000 new cases of oral cancer were diagnosed in the United States, and oral cancer led to nearly 11,000 deaths.3

1. National Institute of Dental and Craniofacial Research (n.d.). Periodontal (Gum) Disease.
2. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (2019). Adult Oral Health.
3. American Cancer Society (2021). Key Statistics for Oral Cavity and Oropharyngeal Cancers.

Date Last Reviewed: April 2025
